1: A type: YHWH commands Moses to ordain Aaron — the priesthood is constituted by divine appointment, not human ambition. Hebrews 5:4-5 cites this pattern directly: “no one takes this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was. So also Christ did not exalt himself to be made a high priest, but was appointed by him who said to him, ‘You are my Son.’” Christ's high priesthood is divinely ordained, not self-assumed.
2: A type: the items assembled for ordination — garments, oil, sin offering, burnt offering, unleavened bread — prefigure the full equipment of Christ's priestly ministry: righteousness (garments), the Spirit (anointing oil), substitutionary atonement (sin offering), total self-offering (burnt offering), and his body as the bread of life (unleavened bread). Each element is gathered because each will be needed in the definitive priestly work.
3: A type: the congregation assembles as witnesses of Aaron's ordination. Christ's designation as High Priest is similarly public: declared at the baptism (“This is my beloved Son”, Matthew 3:17), confirmed at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:5), and proclaimed at the resurrection (Romans 1:4: “declared to be the Son of God in power…by his resurrection from the dead”).
4: A type: Moses, who stands closer to YHWH than any other (Exodus 33:11), installs Aaron. God the Father installs Christ as High Priest by the sworn oath of Psalms 110:4: “The Lord has sworn and will not change his mind, ‘You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.’” (Hebrews 7:21). The Father's oath is the ultimate ordination.
5: A type: “This is the thing that YHWH has commanded to be done” — the formula of divine appointment. Christ's priesthood fulfills every “as YHWH commanded” in the Levitical system. His obedience to the Father's commission was total: “I glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work that you gave me to do” (John 17:4).
6: A type: washing precedes consecration. Christ's baptism in the Jordan (Matthew 3:13-17) is the NT counterpart: though he had no sin to remove, his immersion publicly aligned him with sinful humanity and inaugurated his priestly ministry. The descent of the Spirit at baptism is the NT anointing of the priest (Leviticus 8:12); washing and anointing are the twin entries into the priestly office.
7: A type: Aaron is clothed in the priestly garments. Christ is clothed in perfect righteousness — not as an external garment but as his very nature (Hebrews 7:26: “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners”). Those who believe are in turn “clothed in Christ” (Galatians 3:27), receiving the priestly dignity that derives from his righteousness, not their own.
8: A type: the Urim and Thummim placed in the breastpiece — the instruments of divine disclosure. Christ is the one in whom “all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden” (Colossians 2:3). The need for external oracular instruments points toward the Son himself, through whom God has “spoken to us” definitively (Hebrews 1:2). In Christ, the question-and-answer device is replaced by the Word himself.
9: A type: the golden plate “Holy to YHWH” on the high priest's turban atones for the people's inadvertent offenses. Christ is intrinsically holy (Hebrews 7:26) and bears the people's guilt not on a metal plate but in his own body. The crown of thorns placed on Jesus' head (John 19:2) is a grotesque inversion of this holy crown — the soldiers unknowingly enacted the priestly type: the holy one bears the people's guilt on his head.
10: A type: the anointing of the tabernacle precedes the anointing of Aaron. Christ consecrates the heavenly sanctuary itself (Hebrews 9:23-24: “the heavenly things themselves needed to be purified with better sacrifices”); he enters the true tent “set up by the Lord and not by man” (Hebrews 8:2). Heaven is the space prepared for his eternal priestly ministry.
11: A type: the sevenfold anointing of the altar — complete consecration of the place of sacrifice. Christ's cross is the ultimate altar, consecrated by his own blood; his sacrifice sanctifies all who approach by faith (Hebrews 10:14: “by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified”). The completeness of the sevenfold anointing points to the completeness of his atoning work.
12: A fulfillment: Moses pours the anointing oil on Aaron's head — the mashiach (anointed one) is constituted. Jesus is the Christ (Christos = anointed), the one on whom the Spirit descended at the Jordan “in bodily form like a dove” (Luke 3:22). Peter's Pentecost sermon declares the fulfillment: “God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified” (Acts 2:36).
13: A type: Aaron's sons are clothed in the priestly garments — the priestly order extends beyond the high priest to all who share in his consecration. Christ is the High Priest; believers are “a royal priesthood” (1 Peter 2:9) by virtue of union with him. The sons' consecration follows from the head's consecration — “if the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, so is the whole lump” (Romans 11:16).
14: A type: the sin offering is performed first — atonement precedes dedication. Christ deals with sin before presenting himself as the dedicated priest-king. His death (sin-offering) precedes his resurrection (new-life offering). He could not ascend to the Father's right hand until the sin question was settled — once for all, at the cross (Hebrews 9:26: “he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself”).
15: A type: blood applied to the altar's horns consecrates it as the site of atonement. Christ's blood consecrates the heavenly altar — the mercy seat — as the definitive place of meeting between YHWH and humanity. Paul describes Christ as the hilasterion (mercy seat/propitiation) in Romans 3:25: “whom God put forward as a propitiation by his blood.” The altar's horns that Israelites grasped for refuge (1 Kings 1:50) are a type of clinging to the cross as the place of safety.
16: A type: the fat portions — the richest, most inward parts — are burned for YHWH. Christ offers what is most interior, most essential: his own soul (nefesh) as an offering for sin (Isaiah 53:10: “when his soul makes an offering for guilt”). He holds back nothing, not even his own life (Philippians 2:8: “obedient to the point of death”).
17: A fulfillment: the sin-offering carcass burned outside the camp. Hebrews 13:11-12 reads this as a direct type of the crucifixion: “so Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood.” Golgotha was outside the walls of Jerusalem — the spatial correspondence is exact. The burning outside the camp is the enacted theology of what the cross accomplishes: sin removed, expelled, destroyed.
18: A type: the burnt offering ram — entirely consumed, nothing reserved. Christ's total self-offering: “he loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God” (Ephesians 5:2). The burnt offering has no portion for the priest or offerer — it ascends entirely to YHWH. Christ's offering was similarly unreserved: “I lay it down of my own accord” (John 10:18).
19: A type: blood thrown against all four sides of the altar — comprehensive coverage. Christ's blood covers every approach to God, every direction from which sinners come, every category of human guilt: “he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world” (1 John 2:2).
20: A type: even the entrails and legs are washed and burned — the offering's completeness reaches every part. Christ held nothing back: not his reputation (Philippians 2:7), not his comfort (Luke 9:58), not his life (John 15:13). The offering is comprehensive because the need is comprehensive.
21: A fulfillment: the whole burnt offering rises as a reyach nichoch (pleasing aroma) to YHWH. Paul cites this formula directly for Christ's sacrifice: “Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God” (Ephesians 5:2). The divine reception of Christ's death as “pleasing” declares that the Father's wrath is fully satisfied. What was type in the burnt offering is substance in the cross.
22: A type: the ram of the milu'im — the offering that fills Aaron's hands with the priestly office. Christ's hands are filled with his completed sacrifice: “when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God” (Hebrews 10:12). His hands, pierced at Golgotha, are now the hands of the enthroned High Priest whose ordination-work is permanently and completely done.
23: A type: blood applied to the right earlobe, right thumb, right big toe — consecrating hearing, work, and walk. Christ is the priest whose hearing was perfectly obedient (John 5:30), whose work was wholly dedicated (John 17:4), and whose walk was without sin (1 Peter 2:22). The blood-consecration of the three extremities is fully enacted in his perfect humanity.
24: A type: Aaron's sons receive the same blood application — the priestly order shares in the high priest's consecration. Those in Christ share in his consecration: “you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood” (1 Peter 2:9). The blood that constitutes Aaron's priestly access to YHWH is the same blood that constitutes the new covenant community as a priestly people.
25: A type: fat portions and bread joined in a single composite offering. Christ's self-offering encompasses his full humanity: body and soul, will and affection. He offers himself as both the sacrificial animal (his shed blood) and the bread of life (John 6:35). The composite ordination offering points to the comprehensive character of his self-gift.
26: A type: the three unleavened bread types placed on the fat portions. The unleavened character (no leaven = no sin) points to Christ as the one whose priestly service was untainted: “holy, innocent, unstained” (Hebrews 7:26). The loaves mixed with oil (Spirit-saturated) point to the Spirit-anointed character of his whole ministry.
27: A type: Moses waves the composite offering before YHWH. Christ is the wave offering presented before the Father and consecrated as the eternal High Priest. John 17:19: “for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth” — the ordination wave offering logic, enacted in Christ's self-consecration for the sake of his priestly community.
28: A type: the entire ordination offering is burned — nothing reserved. The founding sacrifice of Christ's priesthood was total. His death was complete; the cup was not partially drunk (John 18:11). The uniqueness of the ordination as an unreserved burning marks the once-for-all character of Christ's founding sacrifice.
29: A type: Moses receives the wave-breast as the officiant's portion. In Christ's case there is no human officiant — the Father receives the entirety. Christ is both priest and offering (Hebrews 9:14), with no division between the one who offers and the one offered. His perfected priesthood is the Father's complete satisfaction.
30: A fulfillment: oil and blood mixed and sprinkled on the priests and their garments. Christ's work provides exactly this for those who are “in him”: the blood of propitiation (atonement secured) and the oil of the Spirit (consecration applied). 1 John 2:27: “the anointing that you received from him abides in you” — the Spirit's anointing is the permanent mark of those consecrated by Christ's blood.
31: A type: the ordination meal eaten at the tent entrance — the priestly community eats the meal of their consecration. Christ instituted the new covenant meal as the ordination feast of the new priestly community: “Do this in remembrance of me” (Luke 22:19). The Last Supper is the NT ordination meal — eaten at the threshold of the new covenant, sealing the community into its priestly identity.
32: A type: no leftovers — everything is either eaten or burned. Christ's body was not left in the grave — the resurrection is the sign that YHWH received the sacrifice without remainder (Acts 2:31: “his flesh did not see corruption”). The offering was complete, consumed, and accepted; nothing remained in the realm of death.
33: A type: seven days at the entrance — the liminal period of the new priestly order. The forty days of Christ's post-resurrection appearances (Acts 1:3) is the extended liminal period during which the new priestly order is established: the disciples receive the risen priest, are commissioned, and await the Spirit. The ordination period gives way to the active priestly ministry — the eighth day (Leviticus 9) / Pentecost.
34: A fulfillment: “YHWH commanded this to be done, to make atonement for you.” Christ's entire life of obedience — from incarnation through resurrection — is an atonement event. Hebrews 10:14: “by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified” — the ordination's atonement-language is fulfilled in the permanent, once-for-all character of what Christ accomplished.
35: A type: day and night at the tent entrance — the priestly vocation is total and continuous. Christ's intercession at the Father's right hand is unceasing: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). The seven-day vigil was temporary; Christ's priestly ministry is eternal — he “holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24).
36: A fulfillment: “Aaron and his sons did all the things that YHWH commanded by Moses.” Christ's obedience was total: “although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered. And being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation” (Hebrews 5:8-9). The ordination narrative ends with perfect compliance; Christ's life ends with the same: “It is finished” (John 19:30).
